one_line_takeaway: VOW makes LLM watermark detection privacy-preserving and cryptographically verifiable via a VOPRF-based two-party protocol with practical short-text performance, but its own experiments show that -- like every other KGW-style scheme it tests against -- it fails to survive paraphrasing by a strong modern LLM (GPT-5.1), reframing robustness as an unsolved, field-wide problem rather than something VOW fixes.
key_finding: VOW achieves strong short-text detectability with formal FPR guarantees: TPR at a fixed FPR of 1e-5 reaches 0.946 at 75 tokens and 0.992 at 150 tokens, performing on par with LeftHash and slightly better than SelfHash, while UPV's comparably fast-rising curve corresponds to an uncontrolled empirical FPR of about 0.17% (vs the 1e-5 target) and PDW needs >=500 tokens (97.40% TPR at 1000 tokens) with no formal FPR control. p-value calibration on 100,000 non-watermarked C4 samples shows VOW (with LeftHash) tracking the ideal uniform distribution closely, while RDF and SelfHash show convex deviation (conservative p-values). On the quality-detectability trade-off, VOW's default (gamma=0.5, delta=2.5) reaches TPR>0.99 with only moderate perplexity increase, versus non-watermarked baseline perplexity of 5.10 and much worse perplexity for RDF (23.68), UPV (25.46), and PDW (1885.12). On downstream utility (Table 1), no-watermark baselines are 81.12% (GSM8K 4-shot) / 54.88% (HumanEval pass@1); VOW(delta=2.5) achieves 76.04%/50.00%, VOW(delta=2.0) 77.94%/53.66% (outperforming LeftHash 75.59%/45.73% and SelfHash 74.37%/46.95%), while RDF and PDW collapse to under 4% on both tasks. On removal-attack robustness (100 watermarked + 100 non-watermarked ELI5/Llama-3.1-8B samples, ~500 tokens), all schemes resist synonym replacement well (AUC>0.95), but paraphrasing is far more damaging: against GPT-3.5-Turbo, LeftHash/SelfHash outperform VOW's default h=4 (VOW h=1 matches them at the cost of weaker unforgeability); against the stronger GPT-5.1 paraphraser, every scheme's AUC/TPR degrades substantially, with p-value distributions of paraphrased watermarked text overlapping heavily with non-watermarked baselines -- the paper's central recalibration claim is that 'no scheme in our evaluation maintains robust and reliable detection against GPT-5.1,' including VOW itself. Efficiency: the unoptimized VOW insertion has prohibitive ITL (429ms at batch size 1, 12,890ms at batch size 32), but the paper's optimizations (rejection sampling + adaptive top-k filtering) cut this by 'over 99.4%' at batch size 32, making VOW the third-lowest-ITL method evaluated; VOPRF batching keeps detection communication overhead at a roughly 13.5x expansion ratio independent of text length. The empirical forgery experiment (500 attempts, cache of 4.5x10^7 unique green n-grams from >100M tokens) produced zero successful forgeries (ASR=0%) with median forged-text p-value 0.225 and very high perplexity (53.05), consistent with the theoretical cache-size lower bound (~9.7x10^10 entries needed).
core_contribution: VOW is a protocol for verifiable and privacy-preserving (oblivious) detection of LLM text watermarks. It reformulates watermark detection within the KGW/Green-Red family as a secure two-party computation: the token 'color' predicate IsGreen(c,t) = Int(F(wk, c||t))/2^l < gamma is instantiated as a Verifiable Oblivious Pseudorandom Function (VOPRF, the 2HashDH construction from RFC 9497). During detection, the User blinds each context-token pair and sends it to the Provider, who runs BlindEvaluate to return a blinded output plus a batched, constant-size DLEQ proof; the User finalizes and verifies the proof before running a one-sided exact Binomial test on the (deduplicated) green-token count to get a p-value. This lets a User/auditor check whether an API response was watermarked by a given provider without revealing the candidate text to the provider, and without trusting the provider's reported detection result. The paper also contributes: (1) a formal threat model for 'trust-minimized' watermarking that separately treats a malicious Provider (who must not learn text content and must not be able to forge results) and a malicious User (who must not be able to forge a passing watermark); (2) practical engineering optimizations to make per-token evaluation of IsGreen tractable during generation (rejection sampling for unbiased multinomial sampling, and an adaptive-cutoff-plus-early-exit scheme for top-k sampling) and during detection (VOPRF batching to make proof size O(1) instead of O(n) in text length); and (3) an empirical re-evaluation of watermark robustness against a modern paraphraser (GPT-5.1), which the authors present as showing that robustness claims for the whole class of KGW-style watermarks (including VOW) have been overestimated relative to prior evaluations that used weaker paraphrasers like GPT-3.5-Turbo.
threat_model: Two-party setting: a Model Provider who holds the watermarking key wk and operates the detection service, and a User who submits candidate text for verification. The Provider is assumed semi-honest during watermark insertion (it correctly embeds the watermark with the committed key, since correct insertion is in its own interest) but potentially malicious during detection: it may falsify results (false negatives to evade accountability for harmful content, or false positives to frame third-party text) or may try to learn the content of the User's submitted text. Required properties against this adversary: Property 1 Privacy -- the Provider's view during Detect must be computationally indistinguishable across any two same-length inputs (i.e., the protocol leaks nothing beyond input length); Property 2 Verifiability -- no PPT malicious Provider can produce a proof that makes an honest Client accept a false detection result y' != y except with negligible probability. On the other side, a malicious User C* does not know wk but has oracle access to the (now-verifiable, hence trustworthy) detection oracle; Property 3, Computational unforgeability, requires that no PPT adversary can construct a text that is not derived from wk yet passes detection, except with probability negligibly above a random text passing by chance. Robustness (resistance to watermark removal/paraphrasing while preserving semantics) is explicitly treated as an empirical, not formally defined, property, evaluated experimentally rather than proven.
limitations: The paper explicitly states VOW does not solve the general robustness problem for LLM watermarking: by design its detection predicate depends on the exact h+1-token sequence, so any single edit (deletion, synonym substitution) produces an unpredictable PRF output and is likely to disrupt the signal ('attack amplification'), and the authors state VOW's robustness is 'comparable to the baseline KGW scheme' rather than improved, deferring stronger removal defenses to future work. There is an explicit unforgeability-vs-robustness trade-off tied to context length h: larger h strengthens the theoretical unforgeability argument but makes the watermark more brittle to edits/paraphrasing (empirically confirmed: VOW's robustness degrades as h increases from 1 to 4). Against the strongest tested paraphraser (GPT-5.1), the paper reports that no evaluated scheme -- including VOW -- retains reliable detection, which the authors frame as a general field-wide overestimation of robustness rather than a VOW-specific defect, but it does mean the core privacy/verifiability contribution does not extend to robust detection under strong adaptive paraphrasing. VOW introduces communication overhead (~13.5x expansion) and requires an interactive protocol for detection (unlike PDW/UPV, which support local/offline detection), so it trades some deployment simplicity for its privacy and verifiability guarantees. Watermark insertion still degrades downstream task utility relative to no watermark (e.g., GSM8K 81.12% -> 76.04%, HumanEval 54.88% -> 50.00% at delta=2.5), and text quality (perplexity) is only 'moderately' increased, not preserved. The unforgeability cost analysis relies on a Zipfian approximation of natural n-gram frequencies and a normal approximation to the exact Binomial test, both stated by the authors as analytical approximations rather than exact bounds; the semantic-coherence argument against caching attacks is presented qualitatively rather than quantified.

datasets_used: C4 (prompts for open-ended generation, detectability/quality/calibration experiments; 100,000 non-watermarked samples for FPR calibration)
ELI5 (prompts for robustness/removal-attack evaluation and empirical forgery attack, with Llama 3.1-8B generation)
quantitative_results: VOW TPR at FPR=1e-5: 0.946 at 75 tokens, 0.992 at 150 tokens
UPV empirical FPR ~0.17% despite nominal 1e-5 target
PDW TPR 97.40% at 1000 tokens (no formal FPR control)
Perplexity at non-watermarked baseline: 5.10; RDF 23.68; UPV 25.46; PDW 1885.12
GSM8K (4-shot) accuracy: no-watermark 81.12%, VOW(delta=2.5) 76.04%, VOW(delta=2.0) 77.94%, LeftHash 75.59%, SelfHash 74.37%, RDF 2.43%, PDW 1.36%, UPV 78.95%
HumanEval (0-shot) pass@1: no-watermark 54.88%, VOW(delta=2.5) 50.00%, VOW(delta=2.0) 53.66%, LeftHash 45.73%, SelfHash 46.95%, RDF 3.66%, PDW 0.0%, UPV 50.00%
Analytical unforgeability bound: attacker needs cache hit rate p_hit > 24.62% at L=300, alpha=1e-5, gamma=0.5, h=4; estimated required cache ~9.7x10^10 unique 4-grams (>=1.9 TB storage)

Empirical forgery attack: 5.8x10^7 cached green context-token pairs (4.5x10^7 unique) from >100M tokens; 500 forgery attempts, 0 successes (ASR=0%), median forged p-value 0.225, forged-text perplexity 53.05

ITL at batch size 32: no watermark 32.95ms; VOW unoptimized 12,890.53ms; VOW optimized (multinomial) 79.77ms, (top-10) 59.94ms, (top-50) 87.78ms; LeftHash 41.20ms; SelfHash 2774.97ms; RDF 33.95ms; UPV 11,103.28ms; optimizations cut VOW latency by 'over 99.4%'
Detection runtime on 500-token texts: LeftHash 118.18ms, SelfHash 265.06ms, RDF 24,075.92ms, PDW 584.20ms, UPV 7.73ms, VOW 62.20ms
Communication expansion ratio for VOW detection: ~13.5x regardless of input length
Removal-attack robustness: AUC>0.95 for most schemes under synonym replacement; against GPT-5.1 paraphrasing, RDF has highest AUC/TPR among schemes tested but TPR only ~25%, still deemed impractical
baselines_compared: UPV (learning-based publicly verifiable watermarking, two neural networks)
